items.question: What is the average-case time complexity of insert/lookup/delete in a hash table?
items.explanation: Given a good hash function and reasonable load factor.

items.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of a hash-table lookup under pathological collisions?
items.explanation: All keys collapse onto one bucket; you scan them linearly.

items.question: What does the term *load factor* refer to?
options: The CPU load when the table is queried
The ratio of entries to buckets
The number of cores rehashing in parallel
The size of the largest entry
items.answer: The ratio of entries to buckets
items.explanation: Load factor n/m drives the resize decision.

items.question: Which collision-resolution strategy does CPython's dict use?
items.explanation: CPython probes a sequence of slots; it does not maintain per-slot chains.

items.question: Why must `__eq__` and `__hash__` be defined consistently?
options: Equal objects must hash equally so the dict probes the same slot
items.answer: Equal objects must hash equally so the dict probes the same slot
items.explanation: If equal objects hash differently the dict will store/look-up in different slots and silently miss.
